articles.title: Two Poets
articles.author: Orson F. Whitney
articles.complete_text:
Two poets there were,
Each sought recognition
And each worshiped his God.
And aid from the press,
One soared to the stars,
Which holdeth the keys
And one clung to the sod.
Such to bane or to bless.

The first sang of heaven;
The second, of earth,
Each offered a master-work,
And men deemed his message
Meant to uplift,
Of paramount worth.
One praising the Giver,
The other, the gift.

A rose-bud, a sunset,
And the sage of the sanctum,
A mountain-girt lake,
With spectacled eye,
More charm held for him
Scanned each offering o'er,
Than the Mighty who spake
And anon made reply.

Unto chaos of old,
To the first: "Too much God
At the birth of a sphere:
In your poem," said he.
"Let the waters abate!
To the second: "Accepted—
Let the dry land appear!"
Your 'Ode to the Sea'."

Then gave to great Nature
And so 'wags the world,
Her infinite dower,
Where the best is deemed worst,
And brought forth all things
Where smallest seems greatest,
By the word of His power.
And last ranks as first.
articles.notes: This is a complete poem by Orson F. Whitney that appears in the misc content but was not extracted as a standalone file. It's a substantial poetic work about two poets and their different approaches to writing.

misc_summary: The remaining content consists of the magazine's table of contents, masthead/publication information, and numerous advertisements for local Salt Lake City businesses including jewelry stores, insurance companies, bakeries, laundries, business colleges, and various consumer products. Also includes the magazine title header.
